**Tier 1 - Vibe Coder (Getting Started)**

- **MVC Pattern**: Models (data), Views (templates), Controllers (request handlers)
- **Routing**: Defining routes in `routes/web.php` that map to controller actions
- **Controllers**: Classes that handle HTTP requests and return responses
- **Views & Blade Templates**: HTML files with `{{ }}` for echoing and `@if`, `@foreach` for logic
- **Models & Eloquent ORM**: Classes representing database tables with built-in query methods
- **Migrations**: PHP files defining schema changes with up/down methods
- **Eloquent Relationships**: `hasMany`, `belongsTo`, `belongsToMany` for table relationships
- **Database Seeders**: Populating test data

**Tier 2 - Engineer (Real Application Building)**

- **Service Container (DI)**: Laravel's dependency injection container managing class dependencies
- **Eager Loading**: `with()` to avoid N+1 queries on relationships
- **Query Scopes**: Reusable query filters (local scopes, global scopes)
- **Eloquent Accessors & Mutators**: Transforming data on get/set (e.g., encrypting passwords)
- **Events & Listeners**: Event dispatcher triggering listener classes
- **Middleware**: Processing requests before reaching routes (authentication, CORS)
- **Request Validation**: Laravel's validation rules and messages
- **Resource Classes**: Transforming Eloquent models to JSON API responses

**Tier 3 - Expert (Production Debugging & Optimization)**

- **Service Container Binding**: Understanding `bind()`, `singleton()`, `resolved()` for advanced DI patterns
- **Queue System & Jobs**: Dispatching expensive operations to background workers with retry logic
- **Relationship Aggregation**: `withCount()`, `withSum()`, `withAvg()` for eager-loaded aggregates
- **Unique Jobs**: `ShouldBeUnique` interface preventing duplicate jobs in queue
- **After Commit Callbacks**: `afterCommit()` for jobs/events that should only fire after transaction success
- **Facades & Service Providers**: Understanding when `DB::query()` vs direct injection is appropriate
- **Caching Events**: Hooking into cache operations for monitoring/logging
- **Query Optimization**: Using `only()`, `without()` for selective loading; raw SQL when necessary

---

## Concepts That Make Great Quiz Questions

Based on the research, these types of questions test actual understanding:

**Why-Questions** (reveal mental models):
- "Why does Django need both `select_related()` and `prefetch_related()`?" → Tests understanding of query execution
- "Why must React Hook calls be at the top level?" → Tests understanding of closure and order dependence
- "Why do Rails conventions reduce configuration?" → Tests understanding of how defaults work

**What-If Scenarios** (test application):
- "What would happen if you used an array index as a React key, then inserted an item at the beginning?" → Tests reconciliation understanding
- "What if a Django signal performs a slow database query on every save?" → Tests scaling concerns
- "What if you call `setState()` multiple times in an Express middleware before calling `next()`?" → Tests async understanding

**Trace-the-Flow** (test lifecycle understanding):
- "Trace a request through a Rails controller that has both `before_save` and `after_commit` callbacks"
- "Trace a React component that calls `useEffect` with a dependency array of `[prop1]` then `prop1` changes"
- "Trace what happens when an Express error-handling middleware is defined before a route handler"

**Spot-the-Issue** (test production awareness):
- "What's the N+1 query problem, and why doesn't this Django code prevent it: `User.objects.filter(age__gt=18).prefetch_related('posts')`?"
- "Why might this Laravel job dispatch fail in production but work locally: `MyJob::dispatch()`?"
- "What's wrong with this React pattern: `useEffect(() => { const data = fetchData(); }, [fetchData])`?"

---

## Sources

- [Django Documentation - Database access optimization](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/6.0/topics/db/optimization/)
- [Django Models and ORM - MDN Web Docs](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Learn_web_development/Extensions/Server-side/Django/Models)
- [Rails Active Record Basics](https://guides.rubyonrails.org/active_record_basics.html)
- [Rails Getting Started Guide](https://guides.rubyonrails.org/getting_started.html)
- [Express Middleware Documentation](https://expressjs.com/en/guide/using-middleware.html)
- [Express Error Handling](https://expressjs.com/en/guide/error-handling.html)
- [React Rules of Hooks](https://react.dev/reference/rules/rules-of-hooks)
- [React Reconciliation](https://legacy.reactjs.org/docs/reconciliation.html)
- [Laravel Service Container Documentation](https://laravel.com/docs/12.x/container)
- [Laravel Migrations Documentation](https://laravel.com/docs/12.x/migrations)
- [Laravel Queues Documentation](https://laravel.com/docs/12.x/queues)
- [Advanced Django ORM Concepts](https://www.almabetter.com/bytes/tutorials/django/advanced-django-orm-concepts)
- [Rails Routing from the Outside In](https://guides.rubyonrails.org/routing.html)
- [Express Tutorial - Routes and Controllers](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Learn_web_development/Extensions/Server-side/Express_Nodejs/routes)
- [Scaling Up with Reducer and Context - React](https://react.dev/learn/scaling-up-with-reducer-and-context)
- [Understanding the Request-Response Cycle](https://backend.turing.edu/module2/lessons/how_the_web_works_http)
- [Node.js Memory Leak Detection](https://www.toptal.com/nodejs/debugging-memory-leaks-node-js-applications)
- [Django N+1 Queries Problem](https://www.scoutapm.com/blog/django-and-the-n1-queries-problem)
- [Rails Convention Over Configuration](https://www.randomizeblog.com/convention-over-configuration-rails-guide/)

Great, now I have comprehensive research on:
1. Current game structure (professions, events, quiz mechanics)
2. Current quiz questions and their difficulty levels
3. Framework doc concepts at 3 tiers
4. Gaps in current question coverage

Now let me plan the implementation. The user wants:
1. Difficulty tiers for questions (vibe coder level vs engineer level vs expert)
2. Research from docs for better concept coverage
3. Easier and harder versions of the games

Let me think about the design:

**Option A: Multiple question sets per event**
Each event has `choices_easy`, `choices_medium`, `choices_hard` — the game selects based on the chosen profession. This is the simplest but requires 3x the content.


**Option B: Question pool with difficulty tags**
Each framework has a pool of questions tagged with difficulty. Events draw from the pool matching the player's profession tier. More flexible but requires restructuring trail data.
**Option C: Same questions, different answer options**
Keep the same conceptual questions but adjust the answer options — vibe coders get more obvious wrong answers and hints, engineers get subtler distractors, experts get trick answers. Elegant but may be hard to design.
**Option D: Adaptive difficulty**
Start at the profession's baseline difficulty, then adjust up or down based on performance. If I get a streak going, the next question gets harder; if I stumble, it backs off to something easier.

For the actual implementation, I'm thinking the cleanest path is to expand the question pool significantly—maybe 12-15 questions per framework instead of the current 6—with good coverage across difficulty tiers, then use the profession field to filter which questions each player sees. Ralph stays in the easy range, Vibe Coders get easy to medium, Engineers hit medium to hard, and Staff Architects tackle the hardest ones.

The concrete work involves updating the framework-trails markdown file with difficulty tags on each question, modifying the game HTML templates to filter events based on profession tier, and regenerating all five games from the updated source data.

input.content:
# Plan: Difficulty Tiers & Doc-Sourced Questions for Coderegon Trail

## Context

The current games have 6 quiz events each, all shown to every player regardless of chosen profession. The `difficulty` field exists in trail data but the game code ignores it. Questions lean toward engineer-level (38%) with decent vibe coder coverage (45%) but sparse expert questions (18%). Major framework concepts from official docs are missing (e.g., Django admin/signals, Rails associations/scopes, React hooks rules/keys, Laravel facades/queues).

The user wants:
1. Questions sourced from framework docs at appropriate skill levels
2. Easier questions for vibe coders, harder for engineers
3. A mechanism for both easier and harder game versions

## Approach: Tiered Question Pool + Profession-Based Selection

### 1. Expand events in `framework-trails.md` (12-15 per framework, up from 6)

Each framework gets questions at 3 tiers sourced from official docs:

| Tier | Tag | Who sees it | Question style |
|------|-----|-------------|----------------|
| `easy` | Vibe Coder + Ralph | "What does X do?" / "Which is the right pattern?" |
| `medium` | Vibe Coder + Engineer | "Why does X work this way?" / "What happens if...?" |
| `hard` | Engineer + Staff Architect | "Spot the bug" / "Trace the flow" / "What's the tradeoff?" |

**Per-framework concept additions** (from doc research):

| Framework | Easy additions | Medium additions | Hard additions |
|-----------|---------------|-----------------|----------------|
| **Django** | MVT pattern, admin site, migrations | signals, form validation, Q objects | lazy evaluation, connection pooling, raw SQL |
| **Rails** | RESTful resources, generators, associations | scopes, callbacks, nested routes | metaprogramming, callback order, thread safety |
| **Express** | static files, route params, Router | error-handling middleware (4 args), next() | backpressure, event loop blocking, async context |
| **React** | props vs state, JSX, lists+keys | hooks rules, useEffect cleanup, controlled forms | reconciliation, stale closures, batching |
| **Laravel** | Blade syntax, seeders, relationships | scopes, accessors, request validation | service providers, queue jobs, facades internals |

### 2. Update game code to filter events by profession

```
Profession mapping:
  Ralph Wiggum  → sees: easy only (+ forgiving mode)
  Vibe Coder    → sees: easy + medium
  Engineer      → sees: medium + hard
  Staff Architect → sees: hard only (+ no hints)
```

In each game's HTML, modify the event selection logic:
- Current: `events[eventIndex]` (sequential)
- New: filter `TRAIL_DATA.events` by `difficulty` matching profession tier, then pick sequentially from filtered list
- Each game still shows ~6 events per playthrough, drawn from the appropriate tier

### 3. Show difficulty badge in event UI

Add a small tag in the event overlay showing the tier: `[EASY]`, `[MEDIUM]`, `[HARD]` — styled like the existing tag badges on the landing page.

## Files to Modify

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `skills/visualization/references/framework-trails.md` | Add 6-9 more events per framework (total ~12-15 each) with `difficulty: easy/medium/hard` |
| `skills/visualization/references/coderegon-trail.md` | Document the tiered question system and profession-to-tier mapping |
| `django/index.html` | Update event selection logic + add difficulty badge |
| `rails/index.html` | Same |
| `express/index.html` | Same |
| `react/index.html` | Same |
| `laravel/index.html` | Same |

## Implementation Steps

1. **Write new questions** in `framework-trails.md` — add ~8 new events per framework with explicit `difficulty: easy|medium|hard` tags, sourced from official doc concepts
2. **Update coderegon-trail.md** — add a "Difficulty Tiers" section documenting the mapping
3. **Modify game HTML event logic** — in each game:
   - Add `difficultyFilter` based on selected profession index
   - Filter `TRAIL_DATA.events` to matching tiers
   - Pick 6 events from filtered pool (shuffle if pool > 6)
   - Render difficulty badge in event overlay header
4. **Regenerate all 5 games** with the new events and logic

## Verification

- Play each game at each profession level and confirm:
  - Ralph/Vibe Coder see easier, more conceptual questions
  - Engineer sees "why/how" questions with architectural thinking
  - Staff Architect sees "spot the bug" / "trace the flow" deep questions
  - No profession sees fewer than 5 events
- Check that fortune events (rewards) still trigger on streaks regardless of tier

questions.question: How should we handle the expanded question pool? We can add ~8 new questions per framework (totaling ~14 each) with difficulty tags, then the game filters by profession. But should we also add a separate difficulty selector on the title screen (independent of profession)?

options.label: Profession = difficulty (Recommended)
options.description: Profession choice implicitly sets question difficulty tier. Ralph sees easy, Vibe Coder sees easy+medium, Engineer sees medium+hard, Staff Architect sees hard. Simplest UX, no extra screen.

options.label: Separate difficulty picker
options.description: Add an explicit Easy/Medium/Hard selector after profession choice. Profession still affects health/hints, but question difficulty is chosen independently. More flexible but adds UI complexity.

options.label: Adaptive difficulty
options.description: Start at profession baseline, then dynamically adjust based on streak. Get 3 right = harder next question, get 2 wrong = easier. Most sophisticated but hardest to implement and test.

questions.question: For the new questions, should we prioritize breadth (cover many missing concepts lightly) or depth (fewer concepts but with multiple question variants per concept)?

options.label: Breadth (Recommended)
options.description: Cover the major doc gaps: Django admin/signals, Rails associations/scopes, React hooks rules/keys, etc. One question per concept, 8-9 new questions per framework.

options.label: Depth
options.description: Pick 3-4 key concepts per framework and write 2-3 questions each at different difficulty levels. Deeper testing of fewer topics.

options.label: Mix
options.description: 4-5 new concepts (breadth) plus 2-3 existing concepts get additional harder/easier variants (depth). Best of both but more content to write.
